design_philosophy.core_loop: Establish settlements to gather resources (gold, lumber, food), train units from buildings, explore map via fog of war, defend against enemies, achieve campaign objectives or defeat opposing players
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management and unit economy balanced against military production; upkeep system penalizes over-expansion, forcing strategic restraint
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command one of four distinct fantasy factions (Human Alliance, Orcish Horde, Night Elves, Undead Scourge) to conquer territories and save/conquer the world of Azeroth
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Introduction of hero units as persistent commanders with individual progression; faction asymmetry where each race has fundamentally different unit rosters and playstyles; upkeep penalty system that actively punishes deathball strategies, enforcing smaller, more tactical armies; three-dimensional rendering that broke from RTS conventions at the time

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Campaign structured by faction progression (Humans, Orcs, Night Elves, Undead), suggesting escalating difficulty; multiplayer allows player-versus-computer matches implying adjustable computer difficulty; single-player campaign laid out similarly to StarCraft with progressive story-driven missions

progression.upgrade_system: Hero units serve as persistent progression vehicles within campaigns; buildings provide unit upgrades and special abilities; resource accumulation gates unit/building availability
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story-driven campaign unlocks subsequent missions; new factions become available through campaign progression; terrain exploration reveals map through fog of war mechanics
replayability_hooks: Four distinct playable factions with asymmetric unit rosters and mechanics
Multiplayer versus computer or other players via LAN or Battle.net
World Editor modding tools enabled community creations like Defense of the Ancients
Varying map sizes and terrain features create different strategic contexts

world_building_techniques: Campaign narrative progresses through four racial perspectives (Human Alliance, Orcish Horde, Night Elves, Undead Scourge)
Story chronicles Burning Legion's invasion attempt and Arthas Menethil's corruption arc
Quest markers and objectives guide players through narrative goals within missions
Character-driven storytelling through hero units (e.g., Thrall as Orc protagonist)
High fantasy setting with established Warcraft universe lore connecting to previous games

notable_bosses: Arthas Menethil (fallen paladin, Undead faction leader, central antagonist)

faction_indicators: Human Alliance (coalition of humans, dwarves, high elves) - order-oriented, defensive
Orcish Horde (orcs, trolls, tauren) - aggressive, primal
Undead Scourge (new faction) - corrupted, apocalyptic force
danger_signals: Arthas's corruption and transformation into undead servant
World Tree corruption as existential threat to Night Elves
Scourge army as overwhelming external threat requiring alliance
environmental_storytelling: Fog of war concealment reveals player knowledge and territory control
Terrain features (rivers, mountains, seas, cliffs) create strategic chokepoints and narrative geography
Building construction and troop movements visible to enemies only when in sight range

sources_used: wikipedia: Warcraft III: Reign of Chaos
